title: The Coding Agent Phase Change - December 2025 Threshold
description: Karpathy describes a fundamental shift in programming that occurred in December 2025: 'coding agents basically didn't work before December and basically work since.' He shares an example of building a complete video analysis dashboard by giving an agent a single English prompt, with the agent working autonomously for 30 minutes to solve multiple issues, write code, test, debug, and deliver a working system. He emphasizes this is not gradual progress but a disruptive phase change requiring new 'agentic engineering' skills.
author: Andrej Karpathy

title: The End of Typing Code - Programming Becomes Unrecognizable
description: Karpathy declares: 'You're not typing computer code into an editor like the way things were since computers were invented, that era is over.' He explains programming is now about spinning up AI agents, giving them tasks in English, and managing their work in parallel. The key skill is building 'orchestrator claws' with tools, memory, and instructions that manage multiple parallel code instances.
author: Andrej Karpathy

title: Build For Agents in 2026 - CLI Interfaces Over GUIs
description: The tech community amplifies Karpathy's message: 'It's 2026. Build. For. Agents.' The insight is that AI agents don't need beautiful buttons and animations - they need programmable, scriptable, deterministic interfaces. After 40 years of GUIs hiding terminals, tools like Claude Code and Codex prefer CLI with pure text, stdin/stdout, and composable pipes.
author: Andrej Karpathy (via @shao__meng)

title: Claude Code Emerges as First Convincing LLM Agent
description: Karpathy highlights Claude Code as 'the first convincing demonstration of what an LLM Agent looks like' - AI that lives on your computer and can write, test, and iterate on code autonomously. This marks a significant milestone in AI-assisted programming capability.
author: Andrej Karpathy

title: Codex for Open Source - OpenAI matches Anthropic's offer
description: OpenAI launched six months of free ChatGPT Pro with Codex for open source maintainers, matching Anthropic's Claude Max offer, though without specifying exact metrics for eligibility.
author: Simon Willison

title: Agentic manual testing - defining characteristic of coding agents
description: The defining characteristic of a coding agent is that it can execute the code that it writes. This is what makes coding agents so much more useful than LLMs that simply spit out code without any way to verify it. Never assume that code generated by an LLM works until that code has been executed.
author: Simon Willison

title: Clinejection - Compromising Cline's Production Releases via Prompt Injection
description: Adnan Khan describes a devious attack chain against the Cline GitHub repository, which started with a prompt injection attack in the title of an issue. The attack combined prompt injection with cache poisoning to steal NPM publishing secrets, resulting in a malicious package release (cline@2.3.0, now retracted).
author: Simon Willison (via Adnan Khan)

title: Anthropic and the Pentagon - AI model commodification
description: Bruce Schneier and Nathan E. Sanders provide thoughtful coverage of Pentagon/OpenAI/Anthropic contracts, noting AI models are increasingly commodified with top-tier offerings having similar performance, making branding and positioning as 'trustworthy' critical for market differentiation.
author: Simon Willison (via Bruce Schneier and Nathan E. Sanders)

title: Cursor Launches Automations - Managing the Agentic Coding Chaos
description: Cursor introduces 'Automations', a system to automatically launch coding agents triggered by codebase changes, Slack messages, or timers. The tool addresses the complexity of managing dozens of coding agents simultaneously, shifting from 'prompt-and-monitor' to automatic agent workflows with human-in-the-loop at key decision points. Cursor reports running 'hundreds of automations per hour' and has reached $2B in annual revenue, doubling in the past three months.
author: Russell Brandom

title: My Policy on AI Writing - Humans Keep the 'I' Pronoun
description: Simon Willison clarifies his AI writing policy: 'if text expresses opinions or has "I" pronouns attached to it then it's written by me. I don't let LLMs speak for me in this way.' He uses LLMs for proofreading and documentation but edits out AI-generated opinions or rationales that LLMs 'just made up.'
author: Simon Willison
